text: You know what, like my team used to operate, and we have stories like that already. My team used to have 30 people doing this thing, now we have two. That is a win for us. Like we can convince them to pay more, maybe we're going to under monetize them for now, that's actually fine. The question is like, you know, can we actually create that value first and do it really well? I think one area that fintech founders are really lucky in with regards to AI, is that fintech is often this invisible monetization layer, like interchange and float. It's kind of invisible to some extent to your end users. Yeah. And so you can just build a product and get away for free and value still accrues you. But for some AI companies, they're automating labor, but do they charge for labor and people aren't willing to pay? Yeah, 100%. And they have to pay out of pocket. And so that's what I'm excited about. You know, and I think the thing is, you know, you compare our business like AmEx, for example, and the retention is so dramatically different that, you know, you sort of get the best of both worlds, right? The retention differentiation of software with the business model and the economics and the revenue drivers of a financial services company. So that's been the thesis since day one, and it continues to be true of AI. I think it just opens this even bigger potential honeypot in the future that we haven't tapped into yet. Yeah, and to talk your own book for you a little bit, one of the reasons I'm so excited about FinTech is you look at companies like Amex, and you're like, even before AI, companies like Brex were starting to take their lunch. Amex's business card business, I think, is actually flat-lined to make sure the consumer is growing. They managed to end their earning costs. That was before AI. How do you think the world is going to be for Amex post-AI? You can slightly decline if you prefer not to speak in love. I'm sure traditional banks are feeling the heat, but it's not coming from us. It's coming from the customer. I think that's the thing that people forget. They're like, oh, Brex is doing this. No, we're not doing anything. The customers are voting at their feet, right? And the way to think about it is, you know, AI, if AI research stops today, there's like 20 years of product development to happen, right? And it's not stopping. So the way we saw it, we see it happening is, you know, as I said, three years ago, we used to pound a table in automation and say, you have to buy automation. This is something that touches every employee in your company. Like you have to automate this process Expenses like everybody is incurring expenses left and right And I think now with AI the really funny thing is like this is coming so much from the top right Boards are telling CEOs, CEOs are telling CFOs, CFOs are done. Everyone in the finance team is going to say, we need to do this with far less people in a far more efficient way. So the tone has changed. And I think the buying criteria is changing, right? Customers are saying, I cannot buy a solution that is manual because, you know, the expectation has just changed. And, you know, there's the Jeff Bezos thing. You know, customers are, you know, always perfectly unhappy. And that is true to some degree. And I think it just widens the gap between us and the banks in a pretty material way. And I think it's going to accelerate over time. I think that point is worth driving home, is my perspective as well, which is even if LLM stopped getting better or there is no new architecture for intelligence that subsumes LLMs, do you feel that the current state-of-the-art is sufficient to deliver a ton of value for customers? Yeah, I mean, I think it's now pretty safe to say that for most asks, if a model can't do something, maybe it's just because you don't have an Eval or an RL case that gets you there. right? So especially reinforcement learning has been something we started to explore over the past few months and it's pretty amazing like how quickly, you know, we for fraud for example, like we, you know, we had traditional machine learning models that they needed like, you know, at best like maybe 10,000 cases, 50,000 cases to generalize and actually learn underlying patterns. And in LLMs, like because you can actually poke under the reasoning hood of the model and with end of 100 cases, you can actually get a model to, and when you actually create good evals and actually you can go and boost the models with whatever you learn about these outcomes, the quality and the performance just materially improve. So RL is something we're very bullish on as well. It hasn't been something that I think most companies have realized yet, but I think there's a lot of meat in that bone. Yeah and it a super simple point but the idea that current state of the art is good enough the incumbents are already starting to lose market share and the pace of product innovation is accelerating it going to be pretty interesting Yeah 100 And the thing that we talk a lot about is like you know Brex is like you know tens of billions of car GMV a year right There 1 trillion of corporate car spend in the U.S. today, growing at 12% a year. So, you know, we're still so small in the grand scheme of things. And one of the things we talk a lot about internally is like, Fintech hasn't happened yet, right? We say, yeah, Brex, we're obviously very proud of what we've built, and there's a lot more to do, but in the grand scheme of things, we have not happened in the United States. The way I articulate it is Fintech is 1% built, and all the Fintech companies basically started with customers banks didn't care about. You went to YC startups as your first customers, and banks would not give them a credit card. And you're like, here's a credit card with a $10,000 limit because you have $100,000 in your bank account. And the banks were like, you can have that. Yeah, exactly. We don't care. We don't want it, right? Yeah. And fast forward today, you're in a different place. A hundred percent. A hundred percent. So we think it hasn't happened yet. And it hasn't happened to a degree that I think banks don't realize. And that's the interesting part when you lay it in the softer side, because the buying criteria for banking will change. And that's the part that it's not going to be a decoupled decision anymore. Yeah, I think it was like almost 10 years ago, Jamie Dime was like, the fintechs are coming. And then 10 years passed and kind of nothing happened. Kind of nothing happened. Like companies like yours were growing and doing well, but they still weren't taking the customers banks cared about. And it's like now you're like, oh, oh, it's starting to happen. Anyway, I'm super excited about the AI for, you know, agentic finance that you guys are building. Also that some of your other like at scale startup competitors are building. But I think really the incumbents are going to suffer the most. And that's where all the market share is. I do also, though, want to talk about some of the other changes that are going on at Brex, which we'll loosely term kind of Brex 3.0. And I think in particular, two things you've done kind of differently relative to the field. One is orientation on partnerships, and two is orientation around a global go-to-market. And so let's start with the global piece first. Why? And this is a question a lot of other fintech founders have to answer is, when do I go global and why? Yeah. Yeah. So, for us, the common thread across these two, right, both global and partnerships, has been enterprise. So when you go serve a very large company a company of like 5 employees 10 employees They almost entirely by definition operate in multiple countries And what we saw is when you went into like a Coinbase for example and you said hey you know, you operate in tens of countries and we solve the U.S. for you. They're like, that's great, but U.S. is solved. I spent 10% of my time in the U.S. 90% of my time is in all these other geos that don't have Brex, that don't have any modern card solution. And guess what? I have employees there that are making expenses. They need corporate cards. They need reimbursements. They need to travel. They need to buy things, et cetera. So one of the things that we've done is we actually are the only fintech in the U.S. that built our entire financial infrastructure from the ground up. So we went straight to MasterCard and built all the payment rails ourselves. So we actually control the entire infrastructure. And what that allowed us to do in 2021 and 2022 is to go take those rails and bring them globally. So we actually operate now in 100-plus countries. So we actually have our own issuing stack from the ground up. So we can issue a local card. It's right there on your website, but I think a lot of people don't realize that you operate in 100 countries. And the benefit is, you know, if you have employees in Brazil, EU, UK, Japan, India, whatever it is, you can have a local experience just like a local bank. And the cool thing is, this sounds really simple, but the reality is it just does not exist. And no one on the planet has built that. Citi sort of has a solution on the enterprise, but it's actually done mostly through M&A, so the systems are still separate. So when you go into an enterprise customer and you say, hey, we have a global solution that gives you local issuing in all of these markets with a single platform, they almost fall out of the chair. They don't believe it's true. And it's actually because of this compounding mode on global payments infrastructure that we've built over eight years. Early on at Brax, we had to do it because there was no Marketo or Stripe issuing. So we had to do it on Rails to launch in the U.S. And over the years, that became a bigger and bigger differentiation. So that was like a very big decision for us. I guess the simplified version of that story is we were moving into enterprise and enterprises were global. Yeah. And so we followed our customers. Exactly, exactly. And then the next thing we saw there was like, well, not only enterprises are going global, all companies are becoming global sooner. So you had a company of 100 employees that said, you know what? I have employees in Canada, I have employees in Mexico, I have employees in Europe. By the way,
